incidents.victim: Ukrainian prosecutors and NATO-country military officials
incidents.vector: Russian hackers compromised email inboxes of Ukrainian prosecutors and allied government/military staff, inadvertently leaving the collection server exposed to researchers.
incidents.impact: At least 284 mailboxes breached between Sept 2024 and Mar 2026, including senior Ukrainian anti-corruption prosecutors, 67 Romanian Air Force accounts, 27 Greek Hellenic National Defense General Staff accounts, and local Bulgarian and Serbian officials.

incidents.vector: Pro-Russian group attempted to manipulate operational technology (OT/ICS) systems at a thermal power plant.
incidents.impact: Intrusion attempt in spring 2025 blocked by built-in facility safeguards; no operational impact. Swedish officials cite similar destructive-minded attempts in Norway, Denmark, and Poland.

incidents.vector: Hackers breached an internal network storing customer data.
incidents.impact: South Africa's largest bank disclosed the breach; it is the third hack of a South African bank this year. Scope of customer data exposure still under investigation.

incidents.vector: Human error during a planned system update caused protected client addresses to be visible inside domestic payment detail screens.
incidents.impact: Inadvertently exposed the addresses of roughly 20,600 Danske Bank clients — many with addresses under legal protection orders — during 2025.

incidents.vector: Two former Kraken employees recorded videos of customer data in the company's backend; a criminal group used the footage to attempt extortion.
incidents.impact: Kraken refused to pay, terminated the employees, and notified affected users and law enforcement. Some customer data was reportedly viewed.

incidents.vector: Data stolen from McGraw Hill's Salesforce tenant during the 2025 wave of Salesforce-targeted thefts and leaked after extortion failed.
incidents.impact: Personal details of about 13.5 million users — names, home addresses, emails, and phone numbers — published publicly.

incidents.vector: Attackers stole data from Hallmark's Salesforce accounts during the 2025 Salesforce-wave intrusions and leaked it after Hallmark refused to pay.
incidents.impact: Personal data of 1.7 million Hallmark customers leaked publicly.

incidents.vector: Breach exposed customer personal data; no financial data reported compromised.
incidents.impact: Customer names, emails, home addresses, phone numbers, and reservation details exposed. Booking.com reset reservation PINs for affected customers.

incidents.vector: SEO poisoning and malvertising drove targets to an adversary-in-the-middle Microsoft 365 login page; stolen tokens were replayed every ~30 minutes via the Axios HTTP client to bypass MFA, after which attackers emailed HR from the victim's account to change direct-deposit details or manipulated Workday directly.
incidents.impact: Multiple Canadian worker accounts compromised; at least one confirmed payroll redirect to an attacker-controlled bank account. Microsoft DART issued guidance and began tenant takedowns.

incidents.victim: SOHO router owners and 200+ government / telecom / energy organizations worldwide
incidents.vector: GRU operators exploited known vulnerabilities to hijack ~18,000 TP-Link and Mikrotik SOHO routers in 120+ countries, changed DNS settings to attacker-controlled resolvers, and used adversary-in-the-middle attacks on Outlook on the web to steal Microsoft OAuth tokens and credentials.
incidents.impact: Espionage campaign affected 200+ organizations and 5,000+ consumer devices, including Afghan government agencies, a European national identity platform, and foreign affairs and law-enforcement bodies in North Africa, Central America, and Southeast Asia. FBI-led Operation Masquerade remediated US-based routers; campaign activity has since ceased.

incidents.victim: US water, wastewater, energy, and government facility operators
incidents.vector: Iran-linked actors targeted publicly exposed programmable logic controllers (PLCs) and SCADA displays, expanding from Unitronics devices to Rockwell Automation FactoryTalk and Allen-Bradley PLCs as the Iran war intensified.
incidents.impact: FBI/NSA/CISA/EPA/DOE joint advisory confirmed interaction with device data, project-file exfiltration, and in some cases operational disruption and financial loss. Unit 42 estimates ~5,600 Rockwell/Allen-Bradley devices globally exposed; Iranian targeting of US critical infrastructure escalated following the Feb 28 US-Israel strikes on Iran.

incidents.vector: Ransomware attack on Dutch healthcare software vendor ChipSoft forced the company to take down its Zorgportaal, HiX Mobile, and Zorgplatform systems.
incidents.impact: HiX is used by ~70% of Dutch hospitals; at least 11 hospitals disconnected ChipSoft from their networks and several patient portals went offline. ChipSoft said it cannot rule out that patient data was accessed; no critical care processes stopped, but rollout of new EHR systems (e.g. Leiden UMC) has been postponed.

incidents.victim: Northern Ireland Education Authority (C2K school network)
incidents.vector: Cyberattack on the centralized C2K IT network that serves almost all schools in Northern Ireland; EA and provider Capita shut the system down to contain the incident.
incidents.impact: Disrupted access to teaching materials, assignments, and exam-revision tools for roughly 300,000 pupils and 20,000 teachers. Recovery ongoing, prioritized for students sitting examinations; EA says no evidence yet that personal data left the system.

incidents.victim: US, Israeli, and Gulf military / energy targets
incidents.attacker: Russia — GRU military intelligence (in cooperation with Iranian state actors)
incidents.vector: Russian satellites conducted at least 24 imagery surveys of 46 military and infrastructure objects across 11 Middle East countries; Russian and Iranian hacker groups coordinated via Telegram, with Iranian groups using Russian VPS infrastructure and techniques.
incidents.impact: Imagery preceded Iranian missile/drone strikes, including a March 27 hit on Prince Sultan Air Base in Saudi Arabia that damaged a US E-3 Sentry AWACS. Iranian groups simultaneously published access credentials to Israeli energy-sector control systems.

incidents.victim: Healthcare, education, professional-services, and finance organizations (US, UK, Australia)
incidents.vector: Medusa ransomware operators rapidly weaponize newly disclosed N-day and occasional zero-day vulnerabilities in web-facing software (e.g. SmarterMail CVE-2026-23760 and GoAnywhere MFT CVE-2025-10035, both exploited a week before public disclosure), moving from initial access to encryption in as little as 24 hours via RMM tools like ScreenConnect, AnyDesk, and SimpleHelp.
incidents.impact: Recent victims include the University of Mississippi Medical Center (fully reopened Mar 2) and New Jersey's Passaic County; healthcare disproportionately affected. Campaign continues to accelerate with ransomware deployment within hours of perimeter compromise.

incidents.vector: Successful phishing attack gave attackers access to client files, later posted online.
incidents.impact: One of the largest US corporate law firms — 2,400+ lawyers and historical counsel to major clients including the Trump 2016 and 2020 campaigns — confirmed hackers accessed client materials.

incidents.victim: Trivy (Aqua Security) users and Iran-based systems
incidents.vector: On Mar 19 the group injected credential-stealing malware into official Trivy releases via GitHub Actions; the same infrastructure then distributed a wiper payload that executes only if the victim's timezone and locale match Iran/Farsi.
incidents.impact: Trivy users had SSH keys, cloud credentials, Kubernetes tokens, and crypto-wallet data stolen before Aqua removed the malicious releases. A follow-on wiper campaign is destroying data on matched Iranian systems, broadening a financially motivated group into quasi-geopolitical action.

incidents.victim: Rogers, Freedom Mobile, Fido (Canadian telecom customers)
incidents.vector: Breach traced back to Canadian business outsourcing provider Telus Digital.
incidents.impact: Three Canadian telecom brands notified customers of data exposure through a third-party provider; scope still being determined.

incidents.victim: BuddyBoss e-learning platform and ~250 customer websites (mostly US universities in the Middle East)
incidents.vector: Attacker directed an Anthropic Claude agent, using stolen credentials, to bypass BuddyBoss's security controls and publish a backdoored update package.
incidents.impact: Nearly 250 customer websites compromised through the poisoned update, the majority American universities operating campuses in the Middle East. Researchers cite this as an early, documented example of AI-agent-directed supply-chain compromise.

incidents.vector: Operators built a fake trading company and met Drift employees at industry conferences to build trust over months, then deployed malware via an Apple TestFlight app and malicious VS Code extensions; compromised devices were used to multi-sign treasury transactions.
incidents.impact: $280 million drained from Drift's treasury. Investigators linked the attack to the DPRK group also tracked as AppleJeus.

incidents.vector: Attackers gained access to NYK's marine fuel procurement platform.
incidents.impact: NYK took the compromised platform offline and acknowledged some employee data was likely stolen. NYK is part of Ocean Network Express, the world's sixth-largest container shipper.

incidents.vector: Ransomware group encrypted the party's IT network at the end of March.
incidents.impact: Die Linke shut down its IT network; employee records stolen, but the party states no member data was compromised.

incidents.vector: Cyberattack targeted the museum's IT systems in February.
incidents.impact: Italy's top art museum relocated its House of Medici collection — including Leonardo da Vinci, Raphael, and Botticelli works — into secure bank vaults. Museum says no passwords or security-layout data were stolen.
